back: UX Goals are specific, measurable objectives that define what a design should achieve in terms of user satisfaction, usability, and business outcomes. They bridge user needs with business objectives.
System Usability Scale (SUS) is a 10-question survey providing a composite score (0-100) measuring perceived usability. Scores above 68 are considered above average.
Net Promoter Score measures customer loyalty by asking how likely users are to recommend the product (0-10 scale). Score = %Promoters (9-10) - %Detractors (0-6). Range: -100 to +100.
Customer Satisfaction Score measures immediate satisfaction with a product, feature, or interaction through direct rating questions (typically 1-5 or 1-10 scale).
The percentage of users who successfully complete a given task without errors. It's a fundamental usability metric expressed as: (Successful completions / Total attempts) × 100.
The duration users take to complete a specific task, from initiation to completion. Lower times generally indicate better usability, though accuracy should also be considered.
The frequency with which users make mistakes while interacting with a system. Types include slip (inadvertent) and mistake (incorrect understanding). Lower error rates indicate better design.

Google's HEART framework measures UX through 5 categories: Happiness (user attitude), Engagement (interaction depth), Adoption (new user acquisition), Retention (existing users returning), and Task Success (efficiency).
UX Targets are specific numerical values or thresholds that goals should achieve by a defined deadline. They make goals measurable and provide benchmarks for evaluating design success.
A method comparing two design versions (A and B) to determine which performs better on defined metrics. Users are randomly assigned to versions and statistical significance determines the winner.
The percentage of users who complete a desired action (purchase, signup, download) out of total visitors. Formula: (Conversions / Total visitors) × 100.
The percentage of visitors who leave after viewing only one page. High bounce rates may indicate poor first impressions, irrelevant content, or usability issues.
A measure of how actively users interact with a product, including metrics like session duration, pages per session, return visits, and content interaction depth.
Research methods collecting non-numerical data like interviews, observations, and open-ended surveys to understand user motivations, pain points, and experiences in depth.
Research methods collecting numerical data through surveys, analytics, and experiments to measure user behavior objectively and identify statistical patterns.
